questions.question: What does the plus sign in "LGBTQ+" include?
options: Only people who are undecided about their identity.
Only people who are lesbian or gay.
Everyone whose sexual orientation or gender identity sits outside what has often been treated as the norm.
Nothing in particular; it is purely decorative.

questions.question: What does inclusive care mean?
options: Keeping conversations about identity to a minimum.
Treating everyone identically and ignoring their differences.
Every person receiving care that is dignified, respectful and shaped around who they truly are.
Giving extra attention only to people who ask for it.

questions.question: How is inclusion in care best described?
options: Something reserved for people who raise concerns.
A generous favour that carers may offer when they have time.
A basic right you owe every person, not an optional extra.
An add-on that applies only in specialist services.

questions.question: What kind of learning does this LGBTQ+ awareness course provide?
options: A substitute for your organisation's safeguarding procedures.
Knowledge-level learning that does not replace policies, safeguarding or supervised training.
A replacement for equality, diversity and inclusion policies.
Supervised, hands-on clinical training that replaces your role training.

questions.question: If this course and your employer's policies differ, what should you do?
options: Ignore both and use your own judgement.
Always follow the course, because it is more recent.
Follow your organisation's policies and ask your manager if you are unsure.
Choose whichever is easier to apply that day.

questions.question: Who does the protected characteristic of sexual orientation protect?
options: Only people who are bisexual.
People who are transgender only.
People who are lesbian, gay, bisexual or heterosexual.
Only people who are gay.

questions.question: The Equality Act's protection against discrimination applies to which of the following?
options: Only interactions with a person's family.
The care and services you provide, not just employment.
Only large organisations, not small care providers.
Only situations involving employment.

questions.question: From what point is a trans person protected under gender reassignment?
options: From the point they decide to live in their affirmed gender.
Only after a doctor has formally confirmed it.
Only after they have had surgery.
Only once they hold a Gender Recognition Certificate.

questions.question: Which of the following is required for a trans person to be protected under gender reassignment?
options: Medical treatment or surgery.
A Gender Recognition Certificate.
Written permission from their family.
None of these; none are required for the protection to apply.

questions.question: As well as LGBTQ+ people themselves, who else does the Equality Act protect from unfair treatment?
options: Only the immediate managers of LGBTQ+ staff.
People who are thought to be LGBTQ+, or who are connected to someone who is.
No one else; the protection is very narrow.
Only people who publicly declare their identity.

questions.question: In what year was sex between men partly decriminalised in England and Wales?
Why might an older LGBTQ+ person be guarded when they start receiving care?
options: They dislike all care workers on principle.
They are usually confused about their own identity.
Past experiences of criminalisation and prejudice can make it hard to trust services and carers.
They rarely have any close relationships.

questions.question: What does going "back into the closet" refer to in the context of care?
options: Staff keeping care records locked away securely.
A person redecorating their home before care begins.
A person choosing to come out to all their carers at once.
Some older people feeling they must hide their identity again when they start receiving care.

questions.question: Sexual orientation is about which of the following?
options: Who a person is emotionally or romantically attracted to.
The pronouns a person prefers.
A person's inner sense of their own gender.
The sex a person was recorded as at birth.

questions.question: Gender identity is best described as which of the following?
options: The name a person was given at birth.
A medical diagnosis given by a doctor.
Who a person is romantically attracted to.
A person's own inner sense of their gender.

questions.question: Which statement describes a trans woman?
options: Someone recorded male at birth who lives as a woman.
Someone who does not identify as a man or a woman.
Someone recorded female at birth who lives as a man.
Someone who is attracted to women.

questions.question: Which statement describes a trans man?
options: Someone who identifies as neither a man nor a woman.
Someone who is attracted to men.
Someone recorded female at birth who lives as a man.
Someone recorded male at birth who lives as a woman.

questions.question: What word describes someone who does not identify simply as a man or a woman?
options: Non-binary.
Heterosexual.
Bisexual.
Transitioning.

questions.question: What is the best guide to the language you should use about a person's identity?
options: The preference of the person's family.
The terms used in old records or medical notes.
The words the person uses about themselves.
Whatever term is most common in the news.

questions.question: If you are unsure which term or pronoun someone prefers, what should you do?
options: Avoid speaking to them until someone else explains.
Guess and hope you get it right.
Use the name on their records regardless.
Ask them politely and privately.

questions.question: What are pronouns?
options: The medical terms for someone's identity.
Formal titles used only in official documents.
Nicknames chosen by care staff.
The words we use in place of someone's name, such as "he", "she" or "they".

questions.question: If you accidentally use the wrong name or pronoun for someone, what should you do?
options: Ask them to be less sensitive about it.
Correct yourself, apologise briefly and move on.
Explain at length why the mistake was understandable.
Pretend it did not happen and say nothing.

questions.question: What is the term for revealing that someone is LGBTQ+ without their consent?
options: Outing them.
Safeguarding them.
Recording them.
Referring them.

questions.question: What is meant by a person's "chosen family"?
options: Members of a formal support group.
The relatives a person sees most often.
Trusted friends and partners who are closest to them, rather than blood relatives.
Care staff who are assigned to support them.

questions.question: If your personal or religious beliefs feel in tension with your duty to care inclusively, what should you do?
options: Share your views with the person to see if they agree.
Talk to your manager or supervisor and continue to support every person equally.
Quietly give that person less attention.
Ask for the person to be moved to another worker without explanation.

questions.question: A trans person does not need to have had surgery or hold a Gender Recognition Certificate to be protected under the Equality Act 2010.
options: True
False

questions.question: Knowing a person's gender identity tells you what their sexual orientation is.
options: True
False

questions.question: Deliberately and repeatedly using the wrong name or pronoun for someone can be a form of harassment.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is acceptable to tell a person's family that they are LGBTQ+ as long as you believe the family has a right to know.
options: True
False

questions.question: A person may be both LGBTQ+ and a person of faith, and both parts of their identity deserve respect.
options: True
False

questions.question: Microaggressions are always deliberate and intended to cause harm.
options: True
False

questions.question: A trans person's previous name should not be shared or referred to unnecessarily, as it can cause distress.
options: True
False

questions.question: Staying silent when you hear a discriminatory remark is the safest and most professional response.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of these are forms of discrimination described in the material? (Select all that apply)
options: Positive encouragement.
Indirect discrimination.
Flexible working.
Harassment.
Direct discrimination.

questions.question: Which of these help create a welcoming, inclusive environment for LGBTQ+ people? (Select all that apply)
options: Avoiding the subject entirely so no one is offended.
Displaying welcoming imagery or symbols.
Using inclusive language such as "partner".
Assuming everyone is heterosexual unless told otherwise.
Making no assumptions about anyone's identity.

questions.question: When providing personal care for a trans person, which of these are good practice? (Select all that apply)
options: Continuing to use their correct name and pronouns throughout.
Asking intrusive questions about their body out of curiosity.
Treating aids such as a binder, padding or prosthesis with respect.
Asking privately about their preferences and following their lead.
Examining or exposing them out of curiosity.

questions.question: If you hear a discriminatory remark, what should you do? (Select all that apply)
options: Record what happened and tell your manager.
Stay silent so as not to cause a scene.
Join in so you do not seem awkward.
Challenge the comment calmly and professionally.
Support anyone who was affected.

questions.question: If a person comes out to you, which of these are good ways to respond? (Select all that apply)
options: Encourage them to come out to everyone straight away.
Tell the rest of the team so they can keep an eye out.
Thank them for trusting you.
Respond warmly and calmly.
Follow their lead about who else they wish to tell.

questions.question: Which of these show a person-centred approach that makes no assumptions? (Select all that apply)
options: Assuming a woman has a husband.
Recording the person's preferences so care stays consistent.
Asking what matters to the person and how they wish to be supported.
Asking about "partners" rather than assuming a husband or wife.
Assuming an older person could not be gay, bisexual or trans.

questions.question: Where can you get advice about supporting LGBTQ+ people or about witnessing discrimination? (Select all that apply)
options: Supervision and team meetings.
Trusted organisations such as Stonewall, Skills for Care and the NHS.
Guessing on your own without checking.
Your manager or supervisor.
Deciding it is not worth raising.

questions.question: Sharing that someone is LGBTQ+ without their consent is called ___ them.
Sex between men was only partly decriminalised in England and Wales in ___.
The Equality Act 2010 sets out ___ protected characteristics.
Pronouns are the words we use in place of someone's ___, such as "he", "she" or "they".
The ___ Information Standard requires health and care services to identify, record and meet people's communication needs.
